mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT a requirement for a valid critical section solution?
mcqs.explanation: The three fundamental requirements are mutual exclusion, progress, and bounded waiting. First-come-first-served is not a requirement; the selection algorithm can be any as long as progress and bounded waiting are satisfied.

mcqs.question: In Peterson's solution for two processes, what do the flag[] array and turn variable represent?

options.text: flag[] indicates who goes first, turn is the process status
flag[] indicates desire to enter, turn indicates whose turn it is
flag[] is the lock, turn is the critical section index
flag[] counts processes, turn tracks time

mcqs.explanation: In Peterson's solution, flag[i] indicates whether process i wants to enter the critical section, and turn indicates which process should go next if there is a conflict.

mcqs.question: What is a race condition?

options.text: When processes run at different speeds
When multiple processes access shared data concurrently and outcome depends on timing
When a process waits for another indefinitely
When the CPU switches between processes

mcqs.explanation: A race condition occurs when the behavior of a program depends on the relative timing of interleaved operations of concurrent processes, leading to unpredictable results.

mcqs.question: The Test-and-Set instruction is atomic because:

options.text: It is executed in software only
It cannot be interrupted by other processes
It works only on single-core systems

mcqs.explanation: Test-and-Set is a hardware atomic instruction that executes as a single indivisible operation. It cannot be interrupted, ensuring that no two processes can execute it simultaneously.

mcqs.question: Which requirement ensures that a process will eventually enter its critical section?
mcqs.explanation: Bounded waiting ensures that after a process requests entry to the critical section, there is a limit on how many times other processes can enter before it gets admitted, preventing starvation.

mcqs.question: In the critical section problem, the 'entry section' refers to:

options.text: The code after the critical section
The code before the critical section where permission is requested

mcqs.explanation: The entry section (or entry protocol) is the code that a process executes before entering the critical section to request permission and wait if necessary.

mcqs.question: Peterson's solution works for how many processes?
mcqs.explanation: Peterson's solution is specifically designed for two processes. It uses two flag variables and one turn variable, which is insufficient for more than two processes.

mcqs.question: What happens when the bounded waiting requirement is violated?
mcqs.explanation: When bounded waiting is violated, processes may starve indefinitely (live-lock) because there is no guarantee on how many other processes can enter the critical section before a waiting process gets its turn.

mcqs.question: Busy-wait synchronization is also known as:
mcqs.explanation: Busy-wait synchronization uses a spinlock where processes continuously check a condition in a loop (polling) rather than being blocked/suspended.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is a hardware-based solution to the critical section problem?
mcqs.explanation: Test-and-Set is a hardware atomic instruction provided by the CPU. Peterson's algorithm and Dekker's algorithm are software-based solutions, while semaphores are OS-provided synchronization primitives.

mcqs.question: The bakery algorithm is designed for:
mcqs.explanation: The bakery algorithm (by Leslie Lamport) is a deadlock-free and starvation-free mutual exclusion algorithm that works for any number of processes (N processes).

mcqs.question: What is the main disadvantage of busy-wait synchronization?

options.text: It wastes CPU cycles by continuously polling

mcqs.explanation: Busy-wait synchronization (spinlock) continuously checks a condition, consuming CPU cycles unnecessarily when the process could be blocked and allowed to resume when the resource is available.

mcqs.question: In the critical section model, the 'exit section' contains:

options.text: Code to enter the critical section

options.code: Code to notify other processes that critical section is available

options.text: The actual shared data access code

mcqs.explanation: The exit section contains code executed after leaving the critical section, typically to release the lock or signal other waiting processes that the critical section is now available.

mcqs.question: Which requirement is violated if processes can enter the critical section simultaneously?
mcqs.explanation: Mutual exclusion requires that at any time, only one process can be in its critical section. If multiple processes can enter simultaneously, mutual exclusion is violated.

mcqs.question: The Swap instruction atomically:
mcqs.explanation: The Swap instruction (or atomic exchange) atomically swaps the values of two variables. It can be used to implement mutual exclusion by swapping a lock variable.

mcqs.question: What is the primary purpose of the 'remainder section'?

options.text: To execute non-critical code that does not require mutual exclusion
To check if entry is allowed

mcqs.explanation: The remainder section is the code outside the critical section that a process executes when not in its critical section. This code does not access shared resources and does not require synchronization.

mcqs.question: When is the progress requirement satisfied in critical section solutions?

options.text: When no process is in CS and some want in, one is selected without delay
When all processes get equal chances

mcqs.explanation: The progress requirement states that if no process is in the critical section and some processes wish to enter, only those not in their remainder section can participate in the selection, and this selection cannot be postponed indefinitely.

mcqs.question: A deadlock may occur when:

options.text: Mutual exclusion holds and processes wait for resources
All processes are in their critical sections

mcqs.explanation: Deadlock occurs when mutual exclusion holds, processes hold resources while waiting for additional resources, no preemption is possible, and a circular wait exists. This is related to critical sections when multiple resources are involved.

mcqs.question: Semaphores are considered a:
mcqs.explanation: Semaphores are synchronization primitives provided by the operating system (OS). They are implemented using atomic operations and provide higher-level abstractions for process synchronization.

mcqs.question: In Peterson's algorithm, what is the value of both flag variables when a process exits the critical section?
mcqs.explanation: When a process exits the critical section in Peterson's algorithm, it sets its flag[processID] to false, indicating it no longer wishes to enter the critical section.
